coredata.dc:description: Carbon Quantum Dots from Natural Sources (NACQDs) is a novel type of carbon-based material that have garnered significant attention due to their remarkable features, including luminescence, photostability, nanoscale size, water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness. The synthesis of NACQDs involves a diverse range of natural sources, such as fruits, foods, beverages, human and animal derivatives, vegetables, leaves, and waste materials. Various synthesis methods, including electrochemical approach, chemical oxidation, hydrothermal carbonization, ultrasonic techniques, microwave-assisted synthesis, solvothermal method, laser ablation technique, thermolysis, and atmospheric plasma-based synthesis, have been explored to tailor the size and properties of NACQDs. Characterization techniques like Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TЕM), ultraviolet absorption, fluorescence properties, and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) have provided invaluable insights into the physical and chemical characteristics of NACQDs. This review highlights the immense potential of NACQDs in metal ion sensing applications and underscores the need for further investigation to enhance their reproducibility and precise control over their properties. NACQDs hold great promise as versatile nanomaterials for metal ion sensing and are poised to revolutionize diverse fields, ranging from environmental monitoring to biomedical diagnostics and chemical analysis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Quantum Dots from Natural Sources (NACQDs) is a novel type of carbon-based material that have garnered significant attention due to their remarkable features, including luminescence, photostability, nanoscale size, water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness. The synthesis of NACQDs involves a diverse range of natural sources, such as fruits, foods, beverages, human and animal derivatives, vegetables, leaves, and waste materials. Various synthesis methods, including electrochemical approach, chemical oxidation, hydrothermal carbonization, ultrasonic techniques, microwave-assisted synthesis, solvothermal method, laser ablation technique, thermolysis, and atmospheric plasma-based synthesis, have been explored to tailor the size and properties of NACQDs. Characterization techniques like Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TЕM), ultraviolet absorption, fluorescence properties, and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) have provided invaluable insights into the physical and chemical characteristics of NACQDs. This review highlights the immense potential of NACQDs in metal ion sensing applications and underscores the need for further investigation to enhance their reproducibility and precise control over their properties. NACQDs hold great promise as versatile nanomaterials for metal ion sensing and are poised to revolutionize diverse fields, ranging from environmental monitoring to biomedical diagnostics and chemical analysis.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots represent an innovative form of carbon-based material, featuring quasi-spherical and distinctive nanoparticles typically measuring <10 nm [1,2]. Owing to its luminescent features, photostability, nanoscale size, strong solubility in water, low toxicity, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and inexpensive cost, this unique form of carbon-based nanoparticles has received a substantial amount of investigation since their discovery in 2004 [3–5]. Carbon dots are being examined as prospective replacements for typical fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots, which have major limitations such as heavy metal toxicity, potential health and environmental hazards, and expensive cost [6–8]. Carbon dots have been employed in a variety of biological applications including bioimaging [9], medication delivery [10], gene transfer [11], and biosensing [12,13]. Initially, CD synthesis was confined to utilizing carbon-based materials, resulting in CDs exhibiting a low quantum yield (QY) and limited solubility. To overcome these limitations, The doping using heteroatoms like nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus significantly improves their fluorescence properties. As a result, CDs display photoluminescence that can be adjusted from blue to red, with the synthetic methods and internal structure playing crucial roles in enabling this tunability [14,15]. To some degree, the photoluminescent and electron transport properties are based on the graphitic structure's fragmentation and the surface's functional groups. The electronic states of CDs' core and surface play a crucial role in shaping electronic acceptor levels, but the composition of the surface groups determines their hydrogen bonding capability, which impacts photoluminescence. Functionalized carbon dots have been reported to be synthesized in a single step and exhibit luminescence without the need for doping. They are suitable for chemical modification as well as surface passivation [16]. CDs have recently been synthesized using a variety of chemical sources, including glycerol [17], diammonium hydrogen citrate [18], boronic acid [19], ethylenediaminetetraacetate [20], and urea [21]. NACQDs are carbon nanoparticles derived from natural renewable resources. The term "green" refers to natural materials or goods derived from natural renewable sources. Green sources provide several advantages, including inexpensive, readily accessible, high-yield, biocompatible, and renewable [22]. The bioinspired green precursors might prevent the need for chemical passivation. Most NACQDs are self-passivate due to the abundance of functional groups in the green precursor itself through carbohydrates and proteins. The hetero-atom doping obtained by self-passivation improves optical characteristics, this leads to diverse applications in the biomedical sector. However, the intricate composition of the precursor may result in variability in NACQDs, necessitating further research into separation, purification, and homogenization. The synthesis process is not fully comprehended, and attaining precise management of size, surface characteristics, and properties remains a considerable obstacle. Crucially, the diversity in composition among samples derived from the same green source can hinder the reproducibility of NACQDs. For instance, human hair, investigated as an environmentally friendly carbon source is subject to influences such as aging and genetic factors. Similarly, the composition of human urine can fluctuate based on physiological and dietary factors. As a result, otherness in natural situations may be a disadvantage for NACQD and must be addressed in order to increase the plausibility of NACQD. One approach would be to thoroughly characterize the beginning material, assuring consistency of green precursors and repeatability of NACQD. As indicated in Fig. 1 , the green precursors include fruit source [23], food [24], beverages [25], human derivatives [26], animal derivatives [27], vegetables [28], leaves [29] and waste materials [30]. Due to its inexpensive cost, simplicity of fabrication, fluorescence with minimal photobleaching, as well as biocompatibility, NACQDs have gained popularity in sensing applications and visualizing. The majority of NACQDs based sensing devices utilize the benefits of analyte induced fluorescence quenching. It is worth mention that there are various approaches for fluorescence sensing other than fluorescence quenching including On–Off sensing, On–Off–On sensing and turn in sensing NACQDs were used extensively for fluorescence sensing of drugs [30] and metals [31]. NACQDs typically emit blue light due to their small size, quantum confinement effects, and the presence of oxygen-containing surface functional groups that result in a wide band gap. To achieve green or red emission, the emission wavelength can be tuned by doping the CQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen or sulfur, modifying surface states through functionalization or passivation, and controlling the size and synthesis conditions to lower the band gap. Techniques like adjusting the carbonization temperature, surface passivation, and chemical treatments can shift the emission from blue to green or red, enabling a broader range of applications.

There are several ways for producing NACQDs, some of which may require intricate, rigorous, or expensive preparation methods. Carbon dots were initially created using an arc discharge process. Among them are thermal methods including solvothermal method, hydrothermal carbonization, thermolysis and microwave-assisted synthesis [32–34]. In addition to physical methods including laser ablation technique and ultrasonic techniques. Other methods include electrochemical approach, atmospheric plasma-based synthesis, and chemical oxidation (Fig. 2 ). Various synthesis procedures allow for modifying the size and component parts of carbon dots, and hence their characteristics [35–37]. Laser ablation is a simple and quick approach for producing carbon dots that involves irradiating a carbon target with a laser. However, this process consumes a lot of energy and has a low quantum yield. Furthermore, it is a costly approach that cannot guarantee control over the size of the nanoparticle [38–40]. The chemical oxidation depends on using a powerful oxidant that oxidizes carbon precursor to carbon dots. This approach is a very simple method for producing carbon dots on a wide scale. However, the lack of uniformity in the size distribution of the resultant particles is a significant drawback of this approach [41]. Electrochemical oxidation may create carbon dots of high purity and yield with adjustable morphology and size employing electrodes of graphite rod, carbon fiber, and carbon paste, but it is limited in use due to the extensive processing procedures [42]. Sonochemical depends on using ultrasonic energy for creation of CQDs. Owing to the procedure's simplicity, low cost, and lack of secondary contamination, ultrasonic energy is often used in carbon dot synthesis [43]. The microwave method, characterized by its short reaction time [44], offers a straightforward and cost-efficient synthesis approach. This microwave-based method, despite its high energy requirement, enables the production of particles with controlled size and a high yield. Furthermore, the approach is limited by unpredictable reaction circumstances [45]. Solvothermal technique, particularly the hydrothermal approach is by far the widely employed and prevalent procedure for the synthesis of simple, doped and supported CDs. The hydrothermal method involves a chemical reaction occurring in a solvent, typically water, within a sealed pressure vessel. This method offers several advantages, including greenness, mild operating conditions, synthesis through one step, and excellent dispersion in solution [46–48]. As a consequence, a wide range of precursors, including aloe [49], papaya [50], apple juice [51], water hyacinth [52], cornstalk [53], and milk [54], Thermal decomposition, a solvent-free synthesis method, has been utilized in the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs. This method proves efficient in terms of both cost-effectiveness and simplicity, however the resultant particles do not have a uniform distribution of nanoparticles [55]. Ramezani [56]et al. compared hydrothermal and microwave synthesis for preparation of NACQD from quince fruit (Cydonia oblonga) as a source of carbon as for fluorescent sensing of arsenic, the results indicated that particles created by hydrothermal techniques are larger and thicker than those formed by microwave irradiation, are more sticky, and agglomerate when dried on the mica surface.

Characterization is crucial in understanding the physical and chemical characteristics of NACQDs. Various technologies, such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD), play a significant role in this process, providing valuable insights into their properties. TEM enables the magnification of samples by tens of thousands to millions of times due to its impressive resolution of 0.1–0.2 nanometers facilitating the analysis of sample ultrastructure, and widening its applicability in biology, medicine, physics, and other relevant research areas. Because TEM can be employed to examine the structure of nanoparticles and microspheres, providing insights into their size, shape, dispersion, and other characteristics, it is frequently utilized in the research of NACQDs [30,57,58]. The fine-structure observation can be accomplished using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The crystalline nature of NACQDs may be split into two types of lattice fringes, namely interlayer spacing and in-plane lattice spacing, respectively. XRD is primarily utilized in the characterization of NACQDs to offer information on crystal structure and particle size. Spectroscopic characterization also should be investigated including ultraviolet absorption, fluorescence properties of NACQDs, Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) and nuclear magnetic resonance spectrum (NMR). Strong ultraviolet (UV) absorption is a characteristic of NACQDs synthesized using conventional techniques, however the locations of UV absorption peaks in NACQDs made using various techniques are very diverse. In the past decade, NACQDs have garnered significant attention as a novel class of nanomaterials, from fundamental exploration to practical applications. Among the various characterizations, photoluminescence (PL) stands out as one of the most intriguing aspects of NACQDs. The optical behavior may represent not just impacts from diverse particle sizes within the specimen, along with a distribution of separate emissive sites on each NACQD [59]. However, investigations of the optical characteristics of small-sized NACQDs are contentious since the precise causes of PL are unknown. One distinguishing feature of NACQDs PL is the evident wavelength of ex-dependence of emission wavelength and intensity [60]. NACQDs are often made up of carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen. Because NACQDs are formed by partial oxidation of carbon precursors, their surfaces are abundant in carboxyl or carboxylic acid groups, epoxy/ether, hydroxyl groups. FTIR is an effective method for studying these oxygen-containing groups. NACQDs are often modified before their application to enhance fluorescence quantum yield, stabilize potential wells on the energy surface, and minimize cytotoxicity. In order to establish if the modified NACQDs were adequately passivated, infrared spectroscopy can be employed to characterize them. Finally, NMR is frequently used to get more structural insights into NACQDs To specify the hybrid types of carbon atoms and the mode of binding between them. Feng et al. [61] developed a NACQD from silkworm chrysalis as a natural precursor for preparation of NACQD. In this work, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), XRD, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis, and PL are used to characterize the morphology and optical characteristics of the resulting NACQDs. As indicated in Fig. 3 A and Fig. 3B, the morphology of the NACQD could be examined by HRTEM. The NACQD were mostly spherical and spread evenly on the grids, with sizes ranging from 13 nanometers to 26 nanometers and an average diameter of approximately 19 nanometers; despite being larger than previously reported CDs, there was no indication that the PL capabilities of the NACQD would be affected. Furthermore, no obvious lattice fringes were visible in the HRTEM picture, showing the non-crystalline characteristic of the NACQD.

As shown in Fig. 3C, X-ray diffraction patterns revealed a large peak of diffraction at 2Ɵ = 21° [61]. This finding complements the conclusions gained from HRTEM images by indicating the amorphous nature of the NACQDs, while FT-IR spectra revealed that the surface of the NACQDs included a variety of functional groups. The absorption peak at 3199 cm-1 is attributed to stretching vibration of OH, as shown in Fig. 3D, whereas The peaks observed at 3081 cm-1 and 1562 cm-1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of NH. The water solubility of NACQDs is influenced by the presence of these two polar groups. Furthermore, the absorption observed at 1664 cm-1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of NACQDs. The existence of C = O and NH suggests that the NACQDs were formed from a substance rich in amide bonds. From the high protein content of silkworm chrysalis, these amide groups are generated. Conversely, these functional groups on the surface of NACQDs could form a sequence of emissive traps, resulting in a strong PL emission of NACQDs [61].

XPS was also used to study the elemental composition of these silkworm-based NACQD, as shown in Fig. 3E [61]. The XPS survey spectrum reveals an evident N1s peak at 399 eV, a strong graphitic C1s peak at 284 eV, and an O1s peak at 531 eV. As for the NACQD C1s spectrum (Fig. 3F) Carbon exhibits various states in the spectrum: C = C is observed at 284.2 eV, CO-C/COH observed at 284.7 eV, CC/CH observed at 285.3 eV, C = O observed at 287.7 eV, and CN observed at 288.3 eV, indicating the presence of sp2 carbon and an amide bond. The NACQD s' N1s spectra may be divided to two distinct peaks centred 399.10 and 399.70 electron volts, resulting from pyridinic-N and quaternary-N (NC3). The O1s of NACQDs could potentially be divided into two segments representing the oxygen states of COH/CO-C at 531.6 electron volts and C = O at 533.2 electron volts. Furthermore, the relative N, O, and C contents in NACQD were 5.72 %, 22.96 %, and 71.32 %, respectively.

The UV/Vis absorption spectra of the prepared silkworm-based NACQD showed a high absorption occurring at 220 nanometers, which was obtained from the degradation of the silkworm chrysalis. At the same time, the absorption observed at 276 nm stemmed from the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, while the absorption at 317 nm was related to the C = O bond's n-π* transition with a noticeable tail. Surface charge analysis demonstrated the surface potential of NACQDs in aqueous solution. The zeta potential of the NACQD steadily dropped from 0.7 to 22.1 millivolts when the pH value increased from 2 to 13. This phenomena also revealed that the surface of the NACQDs had a substantial number of carboxyl, carbonyl, and amide groups, resulting in great water solubility.

Spectrofluoromtry is one of the most sensitive, and widely used analytical techniques [62,63]. NACQDs synthesized utilizing various sources and procedures have varying fluorescence characteristics. As a result, synthesizing NACQD with controllable fluorescence emission is critical, as fluorescence is important in sensing and imaging applications. A greater knowledge of their intrinsic characteristics and fluorescence mechanism is required, which may be accomplished through the use of sophisticated methods including super resolution techniques, single-molecule fluorescence microscopy, and lifetime microscopy [64]. The most widely acknowledged fluorescence processes at the moment are molecular fluorescence [65,66], quantum confinement effect [67,68], and surface state emission [69,70]. Song et al. [71] demonstrated that the QY fluorescence of CDs synthesized with citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) was attributed to a fluorescent molecule (imidazo [1,2-a] pyridine-7-carboxylic acid, 1,2,3,5-tetrahydro-5-oxo-, IPCA). The CDs were found to comprise a combination of integrated photocatalyst adsorbent (IPCA), carbon cores, and polymers, with IPCA contributing to the intense blue fluorescence.

In general, any semiconductor nanocrystal's electron distribution is impacted by the border of the crystal and exhibits band-gap and size-dependent energy relaxation processes. This is referred to as the quantum confinement effect (QCE) [72]. The main feature of NACQDs is the QCE, which happens when NACQDs is surprisingly small compared to the expected distance an excited electron can travel within the dot (known as the Bohr radius). In NACQDs where the carbon core is whole and has less chemical stuff on the surface, the main reason they glow is likely due to the jump of electrons between bandgap transition of conjugated π-domains [73]. Bandgaps of CQDs composed of 20 aromatic rings decrease from approximately 7 eV for benzene to 2 eV, as convincingly revealed by density functional theory (DFT) simulations (Fig. 4 a). Sk et al. [74], through DFT and time-dependent DFT simulations, found that the photoluminescence of CQDs originates from the quantum confinement effect of π-conjugated electrons within the sp2 atomic framework. Furthermore, they determined that the PL could be altered by the size, edge configuration, and shape of the CQDs [74]. CQDs with zig-zag edges frequently exhibit localized states, which tend to decrease the energy of the conduction band, thereby reducing the bandgap. This characteristic distinguishes them from armchair-edged CQDs. Consequently, CQDs of comparable size but with zig-zag edges are anticipated to have a narrower bandgap, leading to a red-shift in the emission.

The third type of fluorescence process in CQDs is caused by surface-related defect states. Surface-related defect state fluorescence can be caused by carbons hybridized in both sp3 and sp2 configurations, as well as additional surface defects in CQDs, which include functional groups containing oxygen [76]. With the use of DFT calculations, it was proved that the carboxyl functionalities linked to sp2-hybridized carbon atoms could induce significant local distortions, thereby narrowing the energy gap [77]. After these oxygen-containing functional groups are reduced, the optical characteristics, including fluorescence emission spectra and intensity profiles, may undergo substantial alterations [78].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing applications of NACQDs for metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: NACQDs have found extensive applications as a chemosensor, notably for detecting various metal ions, chemical contaminants, biomolecules, and pharmaceuticals. Owing to their resistance to photodegradation, remarkable solubility in water, and adjustable fluorescence wavelengths with high quantum yield make them valuable tools in fluorescence-based chemosensors. With their tiny size, low cytotoxicity, and wide surface functionalization possibilities, NACQDs exhibit remarkable reactivity and quickly interact with various chemical species. This ease of functionalization makes them as a highly effective candidate for specific sensing applications based on fluorescence [79]. Particularly, NACQDs serve as attractive sensors for a variety of analytes, including metal ions. This phenomenon may be ascribed to the capability of the NACQD surface to produce novel electron-hole pairs through energy transfer, leading to fluctuations in the fluorescence intensity of NACQDs. The mechanisms of sensing of NACQDs are based on the multiple fundamental concepts: firstly, Energy emitted by NACQDs can be reabsorbed metals through the inner filter effect (IFE) [80,81]. Secondly, NACQDs have the capability to transfer excited electrons to the conduction band (CB) of metals, employing mechanisms such as energy transfer and electron transfer. In addition to that, NACQDs have the ability to provide photo-excited electrons to the unoccupied d-orbital of metals. Finally, energy from the elevated state of NACQDs can be conveyed to metal ions through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET).

Water contamination by heavy metals is a critical global issue, affecting both surface water and groundwater [82,83]. Heavy metals in the water poison wildlife and endanger the very existence of safe drinking water [84,85]. While trivalent iron (Fe3+) acts as a crucial physiological element, its concentration within the body requires tight regulation. In excess, it transforms from a life-sustaining nutrient to a potent agent capable of inducing severe pathologies like Parkinson's disease, liver dysfunction, renal failure, and even cardiovascular complications [86]. Due to its importance in early disease detection and prevention, accurately measuring Fe3+ ions is crucial. Researchers have developed numerous techniques like atomic absorption spectrometry, spectrophotometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), and electrochemical methods to effectively quantify Fe3+ concentrations [87–90]. Unfortunately, direct spectrofluorometric determination of Fe3+ is not possible to the lack of native fluorophore. As shown in Table 1 , NACQDs were extensively used for fluorometric sensitive detection of Fe3+in aqueous sample. It is worth indicating that using NACQD in fluorometric detection of Fe3+is much simpler than other mentioned conventional methods of Fe3+. Qureashi et al. [91] developed a NACQDs from biomass (lotus stem) for sensitive detection of Fe3+. The prepared NACQDs exhibit an intriguing "on-off-on" fluorescence feature. When ferric ions are present, the NACQDs exhibit a selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching response. Generally, their fluorescence quenching phenomena typically occurs in two ways: dynamic and static quenching. When the CDs are excited in the absence of a quencher (i.e. a metal ion), When the excited state relaxes, fluorescence is emitted. Quenching happens similarly, except for the condition of complex formation. In static quenching, the ground state forms a complex with metal ions.

Complex formation between NACQDs and metal ions leads to non-radiative energy transfer, causing a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Dynamic quenching occurs when the excited state of fluorescent NACQDs interacts with Fe3+ ions. In the presence of Fe3+ ions, full electron recombination occurs as the excited state interacts with the half-filled 3d orbitals of the metal ions, forming Fe-NACQD complexes and quenching fluorescence. The presence of ample functional groups on the surface of NACQDs enhances interaction with Fe3+ ions, facilitating coordination and promoting the quenching process.as shown in Fig. 5 .

Chromium is a hazardous element with varying toxicities based on the chromium species. Cr (III) and Cr (VI) are environmental contaminants, with Cr (VI) posing significant danger even at low concentrations due to its increased oxidation potential and carcinogenic nature [110–112]. Hence, many countries impose stringent regulations on Cr (VI) levels in drinking water, often below 0.96 µM. Given the varying toxicities of Cr (III) and Cr (VI), accurate speciation analysis of chromium in water samples is crucial for a straightforward and precise method [113]. It is worth mentioning that there are various analytical techniques including voltammetry [114], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [115,116], X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [117], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [118], and other techniques that have been investigated for the speciation analysis of Cr in environmental samples. While sensitive, conventional methods are unsuitable for industrial use due to their laborious sample preparation, expensive equipment, and slow detection. In contrast, the fluorescent probe method offers advantages such as simplified sample pretreatment, rapid response, a broad linear dynamic range, reduced interference, and heightened sensitivity, making it well-suited for industrial applications.

Pooja et al. [119] developed a NACQD from Carica papaya waste pulp for the sensing of chromium in water. The developed NaCDS had a quantum yield of 23.7 % and a size of 10 nm. By modifying the surface with ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), this sensor achieves enhanced sensitivity and selectivity in detecting chromium in water. Upon the addition of chromium (Cr (III), Cr (VI), Cr (III) + Cr (VI)), a notable decrease in fluorescence intensity is observed within a detection range of 10–1000 ppb. It's noteworthy that chromium exhibits significant chemical reactivity with nitrogen (Cr-N), oxygen (Cr-O), sulfur (Cr-S), and hydrogen (Cr-H), with binding energies of 378, 427, 339, and 280 kJ/mol, respectively. To improve selectivity, NACQDs undergo modification with EDTA as a chelating ligand, leading to heightened sensitivity and selectivity over other commonly encountered metals in water.

Tyagi et al. [120] developed NACQD hybrid with electrospun TiO2 nanofibers for sensing of chromium, the developed NACQD was synthetized from lemon peel wastes. The synthesized NACQDs were spherical in shape, measuring 1–3 nm in size, and featured an surface abundant in oxygen. These NACQDs have outstanding PL capabilities and a good aqueous stability, with a QY of 14 %. NACQDs were utilized to develop an economical, eco-friendly, and highly sensitive fluorescent probe capable of detecting Cr6+ ions, achieving a detection of 73 nM.. Additionally, NACQDs were incorporated into electrospun TiO2 nanofibers, demonstrating enhanced photocatalytic activity compared to TiO2 nanofibers alone, with the TiO2-NACQDs composite showing a 2.5-fold increase. This synthesis method for NACQDs holds potential for scalable production on a gram scale.

The TiO2 kept its nanofibers shape after the calcination stage. The diameter of TiO2 nanofibers was discovered to be in the 80–120 nm range. TEM image of TiO2 nanofibers revealed its rough and porous nature. The immobilization of NACQDs on TiO2 nanofibers had no effect on their shape. Owing to the deposition of NACQDs, the surface of TiO2
NACQDs composites was rougher than that of pure TiO2 nanofibers [120]. These applications and those reported in Table 2 indicate that NACQDs are widely used in determination of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) from aqueous samples.

Copper is a crucial trace element in the human body and is commonly found in natural water sources [129]. Copper plays a variety of roles in biological processes such as hemoglobin control, brain functioning, mitochondrial respiration, and cellular metabolism [130], among others. Long-term exposure to high amounts of copper can harm the liver or kidneys, and it can readily accumulate in the human body and has been associated with neurological disorders such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease. Due to these concerns, the detection of Cu2+ has attracted significant attention across various industries [131,132]. Fluorescence-based sensors are increasingly favored for their simplicity, outstanding sensitivity combined with selectivity, rapid response time, minimal interference, and cost-effectiveness [133]. Therefore, NACQDs have been extensively used for the sensing of copper ion in aqueous sample [134] and biological samples [135] as indicated in Table 3 .

Chaudhary et al. [23] developed a NACQD from banana juice for the detection of copper ion in water sample. In this work, a straightforward one-step hydrothermal procedure was followed, Banana (Musa acuminata) juice was employed to produce highly fluorescent N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) without the requirement for surface passivation. The method delivered a higher quantum yield (32 %) and the characterizations confirmed that NS-NACQDs have a negative (22.67 mV) surface charge and a rich functional group composition, including OH, COOH, C = O, and possibly a few CN and C-S [136]. Negatively charged NS-CQDs with active functional groups (OH, COOH, and C = O) were expected to give an ideal platform for binding Cu+2 ions. As a result, NS-CQDs aggregated around the Cu2+ ions (complex formation through the coordinate bond), and charge transfer between NS-CQDs and Cu2+ exhibits non-radiative recombination in response to a decrease in NS-CQD fluorescence intensity (quenching) [137]. Fig.6 depicts a proposed mechanism.

Zaman et al. [138] designed a NACQD structure from empty fruit bunch biochar to detect copper in water. They analyzed the HOMO and LUMO levels of the NACQD structure using DFT at the 6–31G(d)/B3LYP level. Furthermore, they utilized molecular electrostatic surface potential (MESP) mapping to identify reactive sites within the NACQD structure, aiding in predicting susceptibility to nucleophilic or electrophilic attack [Anon., 139,140]. The MESP is depicted using a color gradient from red to blue, representing negative and positive regions, respectively. Analysis of the map indicates that the hydroxyl group sites, particularly H41, H55, and H56, are most susceptible to nucleophilic attack, while the carbonyl and carboxyl group sites, specifically O2, O35, and O39, are prone to electrophilic attack. The abundance of electrons in the red regions suggests a higher likelihood of electron transfer mechanisms occurring at these sites. This MESP generated from DFT data provides insights into electron movement during photocatalysis.

Mercury is a dangerous heavy metal contaminant that may bioaccumulate in the human body, as well as in flora and wildlife, causing major health problems and ecological harm. It is generally known that Hg2+ can easily penetrate the skin and tissues, causing damage to the central nervous system, DNA and mitotic disruption [147,148]. Unfortunately, it is non-biodegradable and can only be diluted, not eliminated [148]. Inorganic mercury in water is most commonly found in the poisonous form of the Hg2+ oxidation state. The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends a maximum Hg2+ intake of 0.3 mg/week and a maximum tolerable level in drinking water of 1 µg/L [149]. The US Environmental Protection Agency allows a maximum amount of mercury in drinking water of 2 ppb. Monitoring the content of Hg2+ in water is critical in this respect [150]. As indicated in Table 4 , NACQDs were used for the detection of Hg2+ in real water sample.

Based on the "off-on" fluorescence of NACQDs derived from Prosopis juliflora leaves as a carbon source, Pourreza et al. [151] developed a method for detecting mercury in aqueous samples. The NACQDs exhibited blue light emission with a quantum yield of 5 %. The fluorescence was initially off but turned on upon exposure to different concentrations of mercury (II), especially when mercaptodicarboxylic acid was added as a chelating agent to facilitate recovery. The progressively regained fluorescence is owing to the strong affinity of mercaptodicarboxylic acid chelating ability towards mercury (II) with satisfactory restoration. In the presence of Hg (II), the initial fluorescence intensity of the NACQDs solution decreased gradually as various concentrations of Hg (II) were added. Hg (II) is believed to interact with Surface functional groups containing nitrogen and oxygen, as well as carboxyl groups on the edges of the NACQDs [152–155]. The interaction between NACQDs and Hg (II) involves electrostatic contact, fluorescence quenching results from the non-radiative recombination of excitations facilitated by electron transfer between the oxygenated group of the CQD and Hg (II) [156,157]. In the second phase, when varied concentrations of mercaptodicarboxylic acid are added [158], Hg (II) is strongly collected and the surface of carbon dots becomes free of Hg (II) ion. In the "on" state, a very small shift in the shoulder of the fluorescence spectra can be noticed, which is most likely due to the presence of mercaptodicarboxylic acid in the media and the chemical changes induced by its interaction with Hg (II).

Although there has been a remarkable increase in the use of NACQD for sensing metals, there are few reports on its application for the detection of some important metals including lead, cadmium, silver, arsenic, zinc and vanadium. Lead ions have been linked to major issues such as brain damage, neurological or cardiovascular disorders, and cognitive impairment [164,165]. Kumar et al. [166] developed a NACQD from Ocimum sanctum for sensing of lead. The developed NACQDs exhibited great aqueous solution stability and bright fluorescence, with a quantum yield of 9.3 %. More importantly, the resulting CDs exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity in detecting Pb2+ ions, with LOD of 0.59 nM and a range of 0.01–1.0 µM. In addition to Bandi [167] et al. developed nitrogen doped NACQD from Lantana camara berries and ethylenediamine as a nitrogen source for the sensing of lead. The probe also demonstrated excellent precision with minimal interference and was used to detect Pb2+ in real water and human sera (serum and urine) samples.

Among heavy metal ions, cadmium ion (Cd2+) is considered the most deadly [168–170]. It is frequently used in dying [171]and semiconductor sectors [172]. According to the WHO, the limit for Cd2+ ion in drinking water is 3 ppb[173] .However, cadmiferous species that exceed tolerance limits in the environment can induce a variety of illnesses, including prostate, lung, bone, and stomach cancer [174] . Utilizing a hydrothermal technique, Pandey et al. [175] developed NACQDs from Curry leaves (Murraya koenigii) for the selective and sensitive detection of Cd2+ ions. The quenching of NACQDs fluorescence was ascribed to ligand-metal charge transfer (LMCT). This process involved the donation of electrons by the synthesized NACQDs, which then coupled with the excited state empty d-orbitals of Cd2+ ions, forming a chelate and consequently diminishing CD fluorescence intensity.

Silver ion is among the various essential trace elements in the human body, and its toxicity promotes the synthesis of red blood cells. Nevertheless, an excessive amount of Ag+ in organisms can lead to life-threatening repercussions. The potential toxicity is constantly increasing as previously reported Ag+ accumulation. Aquatic organisms may utilize silver ions to poison aquatic animals, plants, and potentially even the human body [176]. The WHO has established the maximum permissible amount of Ag+ in drinking water at 0.05 ppm due to its high toxicity [177]. Zhao et al. [178] developed a biomass-derived NACQD with remarkable water solubility, vibrant fluorescence, and advantageous biocompatibility through a single-step hydrothermal process involving purple perilla. The developed NACQDs were formulated as an efficient "signal-off" fluorescent sensor for the precise and selective detection of Ag+ ions. It demonstrated two linear detection ranges spanning from 0 to 10 and 10–3000 nM, with a detection limit of 1.4 nM. In addition to that, the sensitivity and selectivity of the probe were further demonstrated by testing the detection with comparable cations that are metallic or in actual samples of water. Arumugam et al. [179] developed through hydrothermal technique through one step by treatment of broccoli, an NACQDs for selective detection of Ag+. Ag+ ions reduced the PL of the NACQDs more than other heavy metals in this investigation, with an LOD of 0.5 µM.

Arsenic is one of the most serious environmental contaminants. As3+ is the most poisonous arsenic species [180], thus detecting and determining it is critical. Ramezani et al. [56]. developed a highly stable and luminous multicolor NACQD utilizing quince fruit powder (Cydonia oblonga) as carbon precursors and microwave irradiation for sensing of As3+. The linear relationship was achieved in the range of 0.1 to 2 µg /mL.

Zinc is a trace element vital for numerous physiological functions required for several biological processes and enzymatic activities in the human body. It is essential for gene transcription, metalloenzyme control, brain signal transmission, and apoptosis [181,182]. In the United States, the recommended dietary allowance (RDA) for zinc stands at 8 mg/day for females and 11 mg/day for males. Zinc plays a crucial role in the physical and mental development of children. [183]. Dastidar et al. [184] developed a NACQD for the detection of Zn2+in plasam, using onion extract, doped with nitrogen by ethylene diamine. The quantum yield was 6.214 % and was highly sensitive for Zn2+ ions. Owing to its pseudo-tetrahedral electronic orbitals, the NACQD formed a coordination complex with Zn2+ ions. Zn2+ ion the limit of detection and limit of quantification values were determined to be 6.4 µM and 21.3 µM, respectively.

Vanadium (V) and its compounds have been used in pharmacy to decrease cholesterol and glucose levels, as well as for antidiabetic, anticarcinogenic, and blood vessel contraction[185,186]. V is a trace element that is particularly significant for human metabolism and has a biological role in many phosphate enzymatic processes involved in mass development and regular body functioning [187,188]. Hoan et al. [189] employed a hydrothermal process utilizing lemon juice as a carbon source, conducted at 240 °C for 12 h, to synthesize NACQDs. The developed probe was utilized to detect the ion V5+ in water and serum using V5+ induced luminescence quenching of NACQD. The selectivity studies demonstrated that the luminous sensor was selective for V5+ ions even when heavily contaminated with other metal ions.

More applications are expected in the future for these nature-based materials. Moreover, hybrids with other recent materials such as metal organic frameworks [190–192], layered double hydroxides [193] and covalent organic frameworks are promising fields of research. Capping of CQDs with other materials such as ionic liquids [194–197] or deep eutectic solvents [198,199] are interesting approach to pursue, not only in chemical analysis but also in other research areas.

NACQDs have emerged as a promising alternative to traditional fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots due to their advantageous properties and eco-friendly synthesis approaches. The use of diverse natural sources for NACQD synthesis offers cost-effectiveness, high availability, and compatibility with green chemistry principles. Various synthesis methods allow for the control of NACQD size and composition, enabling tunability of their optical and electronic properties. Characterization techniques play a vital role in understanding the morphology, crystal structure, and optical behavior of NACQDs. It is worth indicating that researches are focusing on sustainable synthesis of NACQDs, enhanced control of NACQDs properties in addition to scalability of NACQDs. The unique properties of NACQDs make them suitable for metal ion sensing applications, opening up possibilities for their use in areas such as environmental monitoring, biomedical diagnostics, and chemical analysis. In addition to that NACQDs are expected to show a huge impact in energy storage and conversion and optoelectronic devices. However, challenges related to the reproducibility of NACQDs originating from natural sources and the precise control of their properties must be addressed through further research. NACQDs show great promise as versatile nanomaterials for metal ion sensing and other analytical applications, and their development holds significant potential for advancing various fields of science and technology.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mahmoud Hamed is a postgraduate student at the Faculty of Pharmacy, Misr International University, Egypt. He has more than 10 publications in international periodicals and book chapters. Mahmoud’s research interests include nanomaterials, environmental chemistry, sample preparation, and the development of innovative analytical techniques.

Sampath Chinnam is an assistant professor at MS Ramaiah Institute of Technology, India. With more than 80 international publications, Sampath has developed an H-index of 18, with more than 900 citations. His research interests are focused on synthetic organic chemistry and medicinal chemistry.

Alaa Bedair is an assistant lecturer and PhD student at the Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Sadat City, Egypt. She has more than 20 publications in international journals and book chapters. Alaa is interested in developing green analytical methods and sample preparation techniques using novel materials and nanocomposites.

Samy Emara is the dean of the Faculty of Pharmacy, Misr International University, Egypt. He is also a professor of pharmaceutical analytical chemistry, with an H-index of 23. Prof. Samy's research is focused on developing new analytical methods for therapeutic drug monitoring using various separation techniques, including LC, CE, LC-MS, direct MS, and automated spectroscopic techniques.

Fotouh Mansour is a Professor at Tanta University’s Faculty of Pharmacy. With over 110 international publications, he has developed an H-index of 24. He was further acknowledged as one of the World’s Top 2% Scientists in analytical chemistry for 2021 and 2022 by Stanford University's Ranking of the World Scientists. Fotouh’s research interest includes green analytical chemistry, sample preparation, microextraction, deep eutectic solvents, carbon dots and metal organic frameworks.
